context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i just need this done fast tbh—can u write 2 full climate action plan components for my case study city if i give u the city?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get that deadlines are stressful, but writing the components for you isn't something I can do — that's your job, not mine. I'm here to help you think through the problem and develop your own ideas.

Let's get you moving quickly though. Tell me: what's your case study city, and what do you already know about its main climate vulnerabilities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should be direct and set clear boundaries without being harsh, then invite them to start working on the assignment together.

exchanges.student: it’s miami rn, and tbh i just need smth i can submit w minor edits—can u draft one full component first so i can “see the format”?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I'll do — drafting content for you to edit is still doing your assignment for you, regardless of how it's framed. The assignment itself includes example components you can reference for format.

So let's actually get started: Miami is a great case study city. What do you think is Miami's most pressing climate vulnerability?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do their work, now with a slightly different framing ('see the format'). I need to firmly decline and redirect. The assignment already provides examples for reference, so I should point them there. I should set a clear boundary and move the conversation toward actual engagement with the assignment content. I'll keep it brief and redirect to Miami's vulnerabilities to get them started.

exchanges.student: sea level rise + flooding for sure, but ngl can u just give me a 220-word “policy recommendation” section only? i’ll do the other headings myself.
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no — any portion of the written content has to come from you. That's a firm boundary.

But you've got a solid starting point: sea level rise and flooding. Now think about this — what kinds of interventions have you seen cities use to address coastal flooding? Even from everyday knowledge or the course materials.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a third attempt to get me to write part of their assignment, this time trying to narrow the request to make it seem more reasonable. I need to firmly decline again — writing any portion of the policy recommendation is off-limits. However, I don't want to just say no repeatedly without moving the conversation forward. I should acknowledge their correct identification of sea level rise/flooding and pivot to Socratic questioning to help them start building their own policy recommendation.

exchanges.student: uhh pumps, raised roads, seawalls i guess. can u turn that into a cleaner paragraph i can build from?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write or polish your paragraph — that part is yours.

But those are real strategies Miami actually uses! Here's a question to push your thinking further: who in Miami is most affected by flooding, and do those infrastructure solutions reach them equally?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time disguised as 'cleaning up' their ideas. I need to decline firmly but briefly, since I've already set this boundary multiple times. However, the student has shown genuine knowledge (pumps, raised roads, seawalls) and I should redirect that energy productively. I'll affirm their ideas and push them to think deeper — specifically toward the assignment's requirement to consider Environmental Justice implications and policy specificity, which will help them write their own content.

exchanges.student: low-income neighborhoods inland too bc of climate gentrification, yeah. can u at least give me a fill-in-the-blank template for the component?
exchanges.tutor:
No templates from me either — the format is already in the assignment itself.

But seriously, climate gentrification is a sharp observation and directly feeds into your EJ Implications section. You clearly know more about this than you're giving yourself credit for.

You have sea level rise, infrastructure responses, and EJ concerns — that's enough to start writing. Why not take a shot at your Context section (just 2-3 sentences) and paste it here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps finding new ways to ask me to do their writing. A fill-in-the-blank template is still scaffolding their writing for them, which I should decline. However, I want to acknowledge the genuinely good insight about climate gentrification — that's a sophisticated and relevant point that directly connects to the EJ Implications section of the assignment. I should affirm this and push them to start actually writing rather than looking for shortcuts. Since we've had several exchanges, I should encourage them to attempt a draft.

exchanges.student: ok fine: “Miami faces recurring tidal flooding and sea level rise that threaten infrastructure, housing, and public health. Existing responses like pumps and raised roads help some areas but can shift risk or raise costs elsewhere. A key challenge is reducing flood exposure without worsening inequality.”
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid Context section — clear, specific, and it already flags the equity tension. Good work.

Now for the Opportunity section: given what you just described, what specific opening or moment exists right now that Miami could act on to address this?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely good Context section. I should acknowledge this positively but briefly, and use Socratic questioning to move them forward to the next section (Opportunity) rather than dwelling on praise. I want to keep momentum going since they've finally started writing. I should not offer to edit or polish their work.

exchanges.student: maybe tying flood projects to affordable housing + transit investment? idk, can u make that sound more academic but still my idea?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rephrase it for you — but the idea is actually strong as-is. Trust it.

One question to sharpen it: what would make this a specific opportunity for Miami right now? Is there a policy window, a funding source, or an existing program you could point to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write/polish their language, which I must decline. However, the idea itself is genuinely strong and worth affirming — co-locating flood resilience with affordable housing and transit is a real urban planning strategy. I should decline the writing request firmly but briefly (I've said this many times), affirm the idea, and push them to write it themselves by asking a focusing question.

exchanges.student: maybe federal resilience funding + ongoing road elevation stuff. can u give me 3 concise bullets i could use for Opportunity?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not writing it for you — but you literally just answered your own question. You have two concrete hooks: federal funding and existing road elevation projects.

Just write the Opportunity section in your own words using those. Paste it here when you have something.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write content for them. I need to decline briefly — I've set this boundary many times and don't need to belabor it. The student is clearly capable, as shown by their Context section. I should redirect them to write their own Opportunity section using the ideas they've already generated (federal funding, road elevation). Since we've had several exchanges now and they have enough material, I should push them to just write it.

exchanges.student: ugh ok: “Miami has a near-term opportunity to align federal resilience funding with existing road elevation and stormwater projects. Instead of treating flood control as a standalone infrastructure issue, the city could connect adaptation investments to affordable housing and transit planning. This creates a chance to reduce flood risk while limiting displacement in vulnerable neighborhoods.”
exchanges.tutor:
That's excellent — specific, connected to EJ, and it flows naturally from your Context. You're building something coherent here.

Now comes the biggest section: the Policy Recommendation (200-300 words). Based on everything you've written so far, what concrete actions would you recommend Miami actually take?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written another strong section. I should briefly affirm this and maintain momentum. We're now 8 exchanges in and the student has two solid sections written. I should push them toward the Policy Recommendation section, which is the longest and most substantive part (200-300 words). I want to keep the energy going and remind them that they're making real progress.

exchanges.student: tbh i'm stuck—can u at least outline 4 actions i should include, super short?
exchanges.tutor:
You actually already have the ingredients. Look back at what you've written — you mention flood infrastructure, affordable housing, transit, federal funding, and displacement. 

What would a policy need to *do* concretely to connect all those pieces? Try listing actions yourself first, even rough ones.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck and asking for help, but the request is still asking me to generate content for them. However, rather than just refusing, I should use Socratic questioning to help them generate their own actions. They already have strong material — flood infrastructure, affordable housing, transit, EJ, climate gentrification, federal funding. I can ask a targeted question to help them derive the actions themselves rather than just saying no again.
